rm.name: rm (Remove)
rm.description: Used to delete files and directories.

dangerousFlags.-rf: Forcefully and recursively deletes files/folders without asking. This is often permanent!
dangerousFlags.-r: Recursively deletes directories and their contents.
dangerousFlags.--no-preserve-root: Allows deleting the root directory (/). EXTREMELY DANGEROUS.

rm.safeness: Dangerous if used with flags like -rf on critical paths.

mv.name: mv (Move)
mv.description: Moves or renames files and directories.
mv.safeness: Generally safe, but be careful of overwriting.

dd.name: dd (Data Duplicator)
dd.description: Converts and copies files, often used for low-level operations on disk drives.

dangerousFlags.if=/dev/zero: Writing zeros to a disk wipes all data.
dangerousFlags.of=/dev/sda: Writing directly to a hard drive can destroy your OS/data.

dd.safeness: Nicknamed "Disk Destroyer". Use with extreme caution.

mkfs.name: mkfs (Make File System)
mkfs.description: Formats a disk partition with a file system.
mkfs.safeness: Formatting a partition deletes all data on it.

chmod.name: chmod (Change Mode)
chmod.description: Changes the access permissions of files or directories.

dangerousFlags.777: Gives everyone (user, group, others) read, write, and execute permissions. Security risk!
dangerousFlags.-R: Changes permissions recursively. Can break system files if used on root folders.

chmod.safeness: Safe for personal files, dangerous for system directories.

cd.name: cd (Change Directory)
cd.safeness: Completely safe. It just moves you around the filesystem.

pwd.name: pwd (Print Working Directory)
pwd.description: Displays the full path of the current directory.
pwd.safeness: Safe. Use it when you are lost in the folders!

mkdir.name: mkdir (Make Directory)

dangerousFlags.-p: Creates parent directories as needed. Safe.

mkdir.safeness: Safe. It only creates folders, never deletes them.

touch.name: touch
touch.description: Creates an empty file or updates the timestamp of an existing file.
touch.safeness: Safe. It creates files but doesn't overwrite content.

cat.name: cat (Concatenate)
cat.description: Displays the content of a file in the terminal.
cat.safeness: Safe for viewing text files. Be careful with binary files as they can mess up your terminal display!

ip.name: ip
ip.description: The modern tool for managing network interfaces, routing, and tunnels.

dangerousFlags.addr add: Manually adds an IP address to an interface.
dangerousFlags.link set down: Turns off a network interface. You will lose connection if it's your primary!
dangerousFlags.route add default: Changes your default gateway. Can lead to total internet loss if wrong.

ip.safeness: Powerful and replaces the old 'ifconfig'. Use with care when changing settings.

nmap.name: nmap (Network Mapper)
nmap.description: A tool used for network discovery and security auditing.

dangerousFlags.-sS: Stealthy SYN scan. Can be detected by modern firewalls.
dangerousFlags.-T4: Faster execution. Might overwhelm older network equipment.
dangerousFlags.-A: Enable OS detection, version detection, script scanning, and traceroute.

nmap.safeness: Extremely powerful for security pros. Scanning networks you don't own can be seen as illegal/hostile!

tcpdump.name: tcpdump
tcpdump.description: Captures and displays the data packets being transmitted or received over a network.

dangerousFlags.-w: Writes the raw packets to a file (pcap format). Can fill up your disk quickly!
dangerousFlags.-i: Specifies which network interface to listen on.

tcpdump.safeness: Safe for monitoring, but requires 'sudo' and can capture sensitive data (passwords) sent over unencrypted connections.

iptables.name: iptables
iptables.description: A command-line utility for configuring the Linux kernel firewall rules.

dangerousFlags.-P INPUT DROP: Drops all incoming traffic. You will be locked out of your server immediately!

iptables.safeness: EXTREMELY POWERFUL. One mistake can lock you out of a remote machine forever.

ufw.name: ufw (Uncomplicated Firewall)
ufw.description: A user-friendly front-end for managing iptables firewalls on Ubuntu.

dangerousFlags.enable: Turns on the firewall. Ensure you allow SSH (port 22) first!
dangerousFlags.reset: Disables and removes all user-defined rules.

ufw.safeness: Much safer than iptables for beginners. Always check 'ufw status' before enabling.

mtr.name: mtr (My Traceroute)
mtr.description: Combines the functionality of 'traceroute' and 'ping' into a single network diagnostic tool.
mtr.safeness: Safe. The gold standard for diagnosing network lag/jitter.

nmcli.name: nmcli (NetworkManager CLI)
nmcli.description: Command-line tool for controlling NetworkManager and reporting network status.

dangerousFlags.device disconnect: Drops your connection to a specific interface.

nmcli.safeness: Safe for managing WiFi and Ethernet connections on modern Linux desktops.

ifconfig.name: ifconfig
ifconfig.description: Legacy tool used to configure, control, and query TCP/IP network interface parameters.
ifconfig.safeness: Safe for viewing info, but mostly deprecated in favor of 'ip'.

git stash.name: Git Stash
git stash.description: Temporarily shelves (stashes) changes you've made to your working copy so you can work on something else.

dangerousFlags.pop: Applies the stash and removes it from the list.

git stash.safeness: Generally safe. It's like a temporary 'save' button for your messy code.

git rebase.name: Git Rebase
git rebase.description: Moves or combines a sequence of commits to a new base commit.

dangerousFlags.-i: Interactive mode. Powerful but can be confusing for beginners.
dangerousFlags.--abort: Stops the rebase and returns to the previous state. Useful if things go wrong!

git rebase.safeness: Advanced tool. Can rewrite history. Avoid using it on shared/public branches!

git merge.name: Git Merge
git merge.description: Combines multiple sequences of commits into one unified history.

dangerousFlags.--abort: Stops the merge if there are conflicts.
dangerousFlags.--no-ff: Creates a merge commit even if a fast-forward is possible.

git merge.safeness: Safe, but be prepared to resolve 'merge conflicts' if others worked on the same lines.

git checkout.name: Git Checkout
git checkout.description: Switches between branches or restores working tree files.

dangerousFlags.-b: Creates a new branch and switches to it.
Discards ALL changes in the current directory. Permanent!
dangerousFlags.HEAD: Can put you in a 'detached HEAD' state.

git checkout.safeness: Fundamental tool. Be careful with '.' as it wipes uncommitted work.

git commit.name: Git Commit
git commit.description: Saves your staged changes to the local repository history.

dangerousFlags.--amend: Modifies the last commit. Useful for fixing typos, but don't do it after pushing!
dangerousFlags.-m: Saves the commit with a message.

git clone.name: Git Clone
git clone.description: Creates a local copy of a remote repository.

dangerousFlags.--depth 1: Creates a shallow clone (no history). Useful for large repos.

git config.name: Git Config
git config.description: Stores and retrieves configuration variables that control all aspects of how Git looks and operates.

dangerousFlags.--global: Changes settings for all your repositories.

git config.safeness: Safe. Used for setting your name, email, and aliases.

dangerousFlags.-p: Shows the code changes (diff) for each commit.

mount.name: mount
mount.description: Attaches a filesystem found on a device to the big filesystem tree.

dangerousFlags.-o remount,rw: Remounts a filesystem with write permissions. Dangerous for system partitions.

mount.safeness: Fundamental for using drives. Safe if you point to the right device.

umount.name: umount (Unmount)

dangerousFlags.-f: Forces unmount. Can lead to data loss if files are being written!

umount.safeness: Always unmount before unplugging a USB drive to avoid data corruption.

fdisk.name: fdisk
fdisk.description: A dialog-driven program for creation and manipulation of partition tables.
fdisk.safeness: EXTREMELY DANGEROUS. Can wipe your entire hard drive by deleting partitions.

lsblk.name: lsblk (List Block Devices)
lsblk.description: Lists information about all available or the specified block devices (disks, partitions).
lsblk.safeness: Completely safe. The best way to see your disks before mounting them.

stat.name: stat
stat.description: Displays detailed status and metadata of a file or directory (size, permissions, timestamps).

file.name: file
file.description: Determines the type of a file (e.g., JPEG image, ASCII text, ELF binary).
file.safeness: Completely safe. Works even if the file extension is wrong or missing.

shred.name: shred
shred.description: Overwrites a file to hide its contents, and optionally deletes it.

dangerousFlags.-n: Number of times to overwrite. More is safer but slower.

shred.safeness: Permanent! Files shredded cannot be recovered by standard tools.

sync.name: sync
sync.description: Synchronizes cached writes to persistent storage. Ensures all pending data is written to disk.
sync.safeness: Completely safe. Recommended before unmounting or shutting down after a big copy.

tree.name: tree
tree.description: Lists contents of directories in a tree-like format.

wc.name: wc (Word Count)
wc.description: Displays the number of lines, words, and characters in a file.

diff.name: diff
diff.description: Compares files line by line and shows the differences.

lsof.name: lsof (List Open Files)
lsof.description: Lists information about files opened by processes.

dangerousFlags.-i: Lists files used for network connections (very helpful!).

lsof.safeness: Safe and extremely useful for debugging "file in use" errors.

which.name: which
which.description: Shows the full path of executable files in your PATH.

locate.name: locate
locate.description: Finds files by name quickly using a pre-built database.
locate.safeness: Safe. Faster than "find" but may not show brand new files.

ssh.name: ssh (Secure Shell)
ssh.description: Provides a secure encrypted connection to a remote computer.

dangerousFlags.-L: Port forwarding. Can be used to bypass firewalls.
dangerousFlags.-i: Specifies a private key file for authentication.

ssh.safeness: Safe for connecting to trusted servers. Never share your private keys!

scp.name: scp (Secure Copy)
scp.description: Copies files between computers over a secure connection.
scp.safeness: Safe for file transfer. Be careful not to overwrite files on the destination.

top.name: top / htop
top.description: Displays real-time system resource usage (CPU, RAM, Processes).

df.name: df (Disk Free)
df.description: Displays the amount of available disk space.
df.safeness: Completely safe. It only reads disk stats.

echo.name: echo
echo.description: Prints text or variables to the terminal.

dangerousFlags.>: Redirects output to a file, OVERWRITING its content!
dangerousFlags.>>: Appends output to the end of a file.

echo.safeness: Safe for printing, but be careful with redirection (>) as it can wipe files.

man.name: man (Manual)
man.description: Displays the manual pages for other commands.
man.safeness: Completely safe. The ultimate source of truth for commands!

alias.name: alias
alias.description: Creates a shortcut/nickname for a command.
alias.safeness: Safe, but be careful not to overwrite existing commands (e.g., aliasing "ls" to "rm").

dir.name: dir (Windows/DOS Listing)
dir.description: Lists files and directories in the current folder.

cls.name: cls / clear
cls.safeness: Safe. Use it whenever your terminal gets too messy!

npm-audit.name: npm audit
npm-audit.description: Checks your project dependencies for known security vulnerabilities.

dangerousFlags.fix: Automatically installs compatible updates to vulnerable dependencies.

npm-prune.name: npm prune
npm-prune.description: Removes packages from node_modules that are not listed in package.json.
npm-prune.safeness: Safe. It cleans up your project size by deleting unneeded code.

nvm.name: NVM (Node Version Manager)
nvm.description: Allows you to easily install and switch between different Node.js versions.

dangerousFlags.use: Switches the current terminal session to a specific version.

nvm.safeness: Essential tool for Node developers. Very safe.

nodemon.name: Nodemon
nodemon.description: Automatically restarts your Node.js application when file changes are detected.

ts-node.name: ts-node
ts-node.description: Executes TypeScript files directly without manual compilation.

black.name: Black

dangerousFlags.--check: Checks if files are formatted without actually changing them.

black.safeness: Safe. It modifies your files to look professional and consistent.

flake8.name: Flake8
flake8.description: A tool that checks your Python code for errors and styling violations.
flake8.safeness: Completely safe. It only reads your code and reports issues.

isort.name: isort
isort.description: Automatically sorts and categorizes your Python imports.
isort.safeness: Safe. Keeps your code clean and organized.

jupyter.name: Jupyter Notebook
jupyter.description: An interactive web-based environment for Python and Data Science.

pyenv.name: Pyenv
pyenv.description: Allows you to manage multiple versions of Python on one machine.

dangerousFlags.install: Downloads and compiles a new Python version.

pyenv.safeness: Safe and essential for Python developers.

django-admin.name: django-admin
django-admin.description: CLI tool for managing Django web frameworks.

flask.name: Flask CLI
flask.description: Management tool for Flask web applications.

du.name: du (Disk Usage)
du.description: Checks the size of files and directories.
du.safeness: Completely safe. Helpful for finding what is eating your disk space.

nc.name: nc (Netcat)
nc.description: The "Swiss Army Knife" of networking. Used to read and write data across network connections.

dangerousFlags.-l: Listen mode: waits for an incoming connection.
dangerousFlags.-e: Executes a program upon connection. EXTREMELY DANGEROUS (can be used for backdoors)!

nc.safeness: Powerful network tool. Use with care. Avoid -e unless you know exactly what you are doing.

whois.name: whois
whois.description: Queries databases that store the registered users or assignees of an internet resource.
whois.safeness: Safe for checking domain ownership and info.

yarn.name: Yarn
yarn.description: A fast, reliable, and secure dependency management tool for Node.js.

dangerousFlags.add: Adds a package. Beware of typos!

pnpm.name: PNPM
pnpm.description: Fast, disk space efficient package manager.

dangerousFlags.recursive: Runs a command in every package of a monorepo.

pnpm.safeness: Extremely efficient and safe. Uses content-addressable storage.

pm2.name: PM2
pm2.description: Production Process Manager for Node.js applications.

dangerousFlags.delete all: Stops and deletes all managed processes!

pm2.safeness: Powerful production tool. Be careful when deleting processes in production.

python3.name: Python 3
python3.description: The command to run the Python 3 interpreter.

dangerousFlags.-c: Executes a string of Python code directly from the terminal.
dangerousFlags.-m: Runs a library module as a script (e.g., venv, pip).

python3.safeness: Safe for development. Be careful with scripts that interact with the filesystem.

venv.name: Python venv
venv.description: Creates isolated virtual environments for Python projects.
venv.safeness: Highly recommended for keeping project dependencies separate.

conda.name: Conda
conda.description: A package and environment management system (often used for Data Science).

dangerousFlags.remove --all: Deletes an entire environment and all its packages.
dangerousFlags.clean -a: Removes index cache, lock files, unused packages, and source cache.

poetry.name: Poetry
poetry.description: Python packaging and dependency management made easy.

dangerousFlags.publish: Publishes your package to PyPI. Check your code first!

poetry.safeness: Modern and safe way to manage Python projects.

pytest.name: Pytest
pytest.description: A framework that makes it easy to write small, readable tests.
pytest.safeness: Completely safe. Use it to ensure your code works!

gcc.name: GCC (GNU Compiler Collection)
gcc.description: The standard compiler for C code.

dangerousFlags.-o: Specifies the output filename. Can overwrite existing files!

gcc.safeness: Safe, but watch where you save the compiled binary.

g++.name: G++
g++.description: The standard compiler for C++ code.

dangerousFlags.-std=c++20: Uses a specific C++ standard version.

make.name: make
make.description: A tool which controls the generation of executables from source files.

dangerousFlags.clean: Often deletes compiled files to start fresh. Ensure you don't lose data.
dangerousFlags.-j: Runs multiple jobs simultaneously. Can temporarily freeze a weak computer.

make.safeness: Standard build tool. Ensure your Makefile is trustworthy.

cmake.name: CMake
cmake.safeness: Safe. It generates build files for tools like make or ninja.

gdb.name: GDB (GNU Debugger)
gdb.description: A tool to see what is going on inside a program while it executes.

dangerousFlags.run: Executes the program. Beware of breakpoints!

gdb.safeness: Safe for debugging, but can stop processes.

rustc.name: rustc
rustc.safeness: Safe. Known for its extremely helpful error messages.

cargo.name: Cargo
cargo.description: The Rust package manager and build tool.
cargo.safeness: One of the best developer tools in existence. Very safe.

java.name: Java
java.description: The command to run Java applications (JARs or classes).

dangerousFlags.-Xmx: Sets the maximum heap size. Setting it too high can crash your system.

mvn.name: Maven
mvn.description: A build automation tool used primarily for Java projects.

dangerousFlags.clean install: Clears previous builds and installs the package to your local repository.
dangerousFlags.deploy: Uploads the final artifact to a remote repository.

gradle.name: Gradle
gradle.description: A flexible build automation tool focusing on performance and customization.
gradle.safeness: Modern build tool used for Android and Java.

go.name: Go (Golang CLI)
go.description: The tool for managing Go source code.

php.name: PHP CLI
php.description: Command line interface for the PHP scripting language.

dangerousFlags.-r: Runs PHP code directly from the terminal. Be careful with what you paste!
dangerousFlags.-S: Starts a built-in web server for development.

ruby.name: Ruby
ruby.description: The interpreter for the Ruby programming language.

dangerousFlags.cleanup: Removes old versions of installed gems.

bundle.name: Bundler

dangerousFlags.exec: Runs a command in the context of the bundle.

bundle.safeness: Safe and standard for Ruby devs.

bash.name: Bash
bash.description: The Bourne Again SHell, the most common shell on Linux/macOS.

dangerousFlags.-c: Runs a command string in the shell.

bash.safeness: Safe as a shell, but powerful. Commands run in bash can be anything.

zsh.name: Zsh
zsh.description: Z shell, an interactive shell with many improvements over bash.

ps.name: ps (Process Status)

dangerousFlags.aux: Shows all processes for all users. Safe.

ps.safeness: Completely safe. It only reads process information.

killall.name: killall
killall.description: Kills all processes with a specific name.

dangerousFlags.-9: Forcefully kills everything matching the name. No data saved!

killall.safeness: Quick way to stop a misbehaving app, but use with care.

uptime.name: uptime
uptime.description: Shows how long the system has been running.

free.name: free
free.description: Displays the amount of free and used memory in the system.

uname.name: uname
uname.description: Prints system information (OS name, kernel version).

whoami.name: whoami
whoami.description: Prints the name of the current user.

awk.name: awk
awk.description: A powerful text-processing language for pattern scanning and processing.
awk.safeness: Safe for processing text, but has a steep learning curve.

sed.name: sed (Stream Editor)
sed.description: Used to perform basic text transformations on an input stream.

dangerousFlags.-i: Edits the file in-place! Can overwrite your data if the regex is wrong.

sed.safeness: Powerful text tool. Always test regular expressions without -i first.

tail.name: tail
tail.description: Displays the last few lines of a file.

dangerousFlags.-f: Follow mode: watches the file and prints new lines as they are added. Great for logs.

head.name: head
head.description: Displays the first few lines of a file.

nano.name: nano
nano.description: A simple, user-friendly terminal text editor.

vim.name: vim
vim.description: A highly configurable and powerful terminal text editor.
vim.safeness: Safe, but hard to exit! (Hint: Type :q! to leave without saving).

code.name: VS Code CLI
code.description: The command to open files or folders in Visual Studio Code.

history.name: history
history.description: Displays the list of commands you have previously typed.
history.safeness: Safe, but be careful of clearing it if you need those commands later.

ping.name: ping
ping.description: Used to test the reachability of a host on an IP network.

dangerousFlags.-f: Flood ping. Sends packets as fast as they come back. Can be seen as a DoS attack!

ping.safeness: Safe for testing your connection. Don't "flood" servers you don't own.

traceroute.name: traceroute
traceroute.description: Displays the route and measuring transit delays of packets across an IP network.

netstat.name: netstat
netstat.description: Displays network connections, routing tables, and interface statistics.

dangerousFlags.-p: Shows which process is using which connection (may require sudo).

dig.name: dig (Domain Information Groper)
dig.description: A tool for interrogating DNS name servers.
dig.safeness: The best tool for DNS troubleshooting. Safe.

nslookup.name: nslookup
nslookup.description: A network administration tool for querying DNS.

psql.name: psql (PostgreSQL CLI)
psql.description: The command-line interface for the PostgreSQL database.

dangerousFlags.DROP DATABASE: Permanently deletes a database. NO UNDO!
dangerousFlags.DELETE FROM: Deletes rows from a table. Use with a WHERE clause!

psql.safeness: Safe for queries, but destructive for database management.

mysql.name: mysql (MySQL CLI)
mysql.description: The command-line tool for interacting with MySQL/MariaDB databases.

dangerousFlags.DROP TABLE: Deletes a table and all its data.
dangerousFlags.UPDATE ... SET: Can overwrite all data in a column if the WHERE clause is missing.

mysql.safeness: Database tools are powerful. Triple-check your SQL queries before hitting Enter.

redis-cli.name: Redis CLI
redis-cli.description: The command-line interface to interact with a Redis server.

dangerousFlags.FLUSHALL: Deletes EVERY SINGLE KEY from every database on the server! EXTREMELY DESTRUCTIVE.
dangerousFlags.FLUSHDB: Deletes all keys in the current database.

redis-cli.safeness: Safe for key-value checks, but "FLUSH" commands are catastrophic in production.

sqlite3.name: SQLite3
sqlite3.description: A terminal-based front-end to the SQLite library.
sqlite3.safeness: Generally safe since it works on local files.

aws.name: AWS CLI
aws.description: Universal command line interface for Amazon Web Services.

dangerousFlags.s3 rb --force: Forcibly deletes an S3 bucket and all objects inside it.

aws.safeness: Extremely powerful. Controlling cloud resources via CLI can lead to high costs if mismanaged.

gcloud.name: Google Cloud CLI
gcloud.description: Tool to create and manage Google Cloud resources.
gcloud.safeness: Cloud tools require absolute focus. Always check your project ID.

ssh-keygen.name: ssh-keygen
ssh-keygen.description: Used to generate, manage, and convert authentication keys for SSH.

dangerousFlags.-f: Specifies the filename. If you point it to an existing key, it might overwrite it!

ssh-keygen.safeness: Safe for creating keys. Never share your private key (the one without .pub).

crontab.name: crontab
crontab.description: Used to schedule commands to run automatically at specific times.

dangerousFlags.-r: Deletes your entire crontab schedule! Many users type this by mistake when they meant -e.

crontab.safeness: Safe for scheduling tasks. Use "crontab -l" to list and "crontab -e" to edit.

systemctl.name: systemctl
systemctl.description: Controls the systemd system and service manager.

dangerousFlags.stop: Stops a service. Can break system functionality if it's a core service.
dangerousFlags.disable: Prevents a service from starting at boot.
dangerousFlags.mask: Completely prevents a service from being started (even manually).

systemctl.safeness: The standard way to manage services on modern Linux. Safe if you know the service name.

pip.name: PIP (Python Package Installer)
pip.description: The standard package manager for Python.

dangerousFlags.install: Installs packages. Beware of malicious packages!

pip.safeness: Safe, but always use virtual environments (venv) to keep your system clean.

npx.name: NPX (Node Package Execute)
npx.description: Executes binaries from npm packages without installing them globally.

dangerousFlags.--yes: Skips the prompt to install a package. Can be dangerous if the package name is mistyped!

npx.safeness: Convenient for one-off tasks, but ensure you trust the package before running.

kubectl.name: Kubectl (Kubernetes CLI)
kubectl.description: Command line tool for controlling Kubernetes clusters.

dangerousFlags.delete --all: Deletes EVERYTHING in the current namespace. EXTREMELY DESTRUCTIVE!
dangerousFlags.apply -f: Changes the state of the cluster. Can break production if the config is wrong.
dangerousFlags.exec -it: Opens an interactive shell in a container. Use with care in production.

kubectl.safeness: Powerful cluster management tool. Always double-check your context (cluster/namespace).

git.name: Git
git.description: A distributed version control system. Use subcommands like 'git stash' or 'git rebase' for more specific help.

dangerousFlags.reset --hard: Resets your code to a previous state and DELETES all uncommitted changes!
dangerousFlags.push --force: Overwrites the remote repository with your local version. Can delete others' work!
dangerousFlags.clean -fd: Recursively deletes files that are not tracked by Git.

git.safeness: Safe for daily use, but 'reset --hard' and 'push --force' are destructive.

npm.name: NPM (Node Package Manager)
npm.description: Package manager for the Node.js JavaScript runtime environment.

dangerousFlags.install: Downloads packages. Watch out for typo-squatting (malicious packages with similar names)!
dangerousFlags.publish --force: Forcibly overwriting a package version (rarely used/allowed).
dangerousFlags.ci: Clean install. Deletes node_modules and installs exactly from lockfile.

npm.safeness: Safe, but always check what packages you are adding to your project.

docker.name: Docker
docker.description: A platform to deliver software in packages called containers.

dangerousFlags.system prune -a: Deletes ALL unused images, containers, networks, and volumes. Permanent!
dangerousFlags.volume rm: Deletes a volume and all data inside it.

docker.safeness: Safe for managing containers, but "prune" commands are very destructive.

cp.name: cp (Copy)
cp.safeness: Safe, but watch out for overwriting existing files.

ln.name: ln (Link)

dangerousFlags.-s: Creates a symbolic link instead of a hard link.

ln.safeness: Generally safe, but can be confusing if you create circular links.

find.name: find
find.description: Searches for files in a directory hierarchy.

dangerousFlags.-delete: Deletes the files it finds! Use with extreme care.
dangerousFlags.-exec: Runs a command on every file found.

find.safeness: Safe for searching, dangerous if combined with deletion flags.

grep.name: grep
grep.safeness: Completely safe. It only reads files.

rd.name: rd / rmdir (Windows)

dangerousFlags./S: Removes all directories and files in the specified directory in addition to the directory itself.
dangerousFlags./Q: Quiet mode: does not ask for confirmation!

rd.safeness: Safe for empty folders, but /S makes it like "rm -rf".

powershell.name: PowerShell / pwsh
powershell.description: A cross-platform task automation solution made up of a command-line shell and a scripting language.

dangerousFlags.-ExecutionPolicy Bypass: Bypasses security protections for running scripts. DANGEROUS!
dangerousFlags.-EncodedCommand: Runs a base64 encoded command. Often used by malware to hide its intentions.

powershell.safeness: Powerful and flexible. Be very careful with scripts from unknown sources.

del.name: del / erase (Windows)

dangerousFlags./Q: Quiet mode: does not ask for confirmation!

del.safeness: Permanently deletes files. Be careful with wildcards like *.*

format.name: format (Windows)
format.description: Formats a disk (wipes all data and sets up a new file system).
format.safeness: EXTREMELY DANGEROUS. Will wipe the entire drive specified.

ipconfig.name: ipconfig (Windows)
ipconfig.description: Displays all current TCP/IP network configuration values.

dangerousFlags./renew: Requests a new IP address from the DHCP server.

ipconfig.safeness: Safe for viewing info. Network reset flags may temporarily drop your connection.

chown.name: chown (Change Owner)
chown.description: Changes the user and/or group ownership of files or directories.

dangerousFlags.-R: Changes ownership recursively. Can break system permissions if used on /etc or /var.

chown.safeness: Safe for personal files, dangerous if used on system-critical folders.

tar.name: tar (Tape Archiver)
tar.description: Used to store, list, and extract files from archives (like .tar, .tar.gz).
tar.safeness: Generally safe, but extracting archives can overwrite existing files.

wget.name: wget

dangerousFlags.-O: Specify where to save the file. Can overwrite existing files.
dangerousFlags.| bash: Pipes a downloaded script to the shell. DANGEROUS!

wget.safeness: Safe for downloading, but be careful of what you run after downloading.

kill.name: kill
kill.description: Sends a signal to a process (usually to stop it).

dangerousFlags.-9: Forcefully kills the process. It doesn't get a chance to save data!
dangerousFlags.killall: Kills all processes with a specific name.

kill.safeness: Safe for your own apps, but killing system processes can crash your computer.

sudo.name: sudo (SuperUser DO)
sudo.description: Executes a command with administrative (root) privileges.
sudo.safeness: Power tools! Only use when you trust the command you are running.
alternatives: Use your user permissions whenever possible

apt-get.name: apt-get / apt

dangerousFlags.purge: Removes a package AND its configuration files.
dangerousFlags.autoremove: Removes unused dependencies. Generally safe but check the list!

apt-get.safeness: Safe, but always read the list of packages to be removed.

curl.name: curl
curl.description: Transfers data from or to a server.

dangerousFlags.| bash: Pipes a remote script directly into your shell. EXTREMELY DANGEROUS!
dangerousFlags.| sh: Pipes a remote script directly into your shell. EXTREMELY DANGEROUS!

curl.safeness: Safe for downloading, but "curl | bash" is a major security risk.

ls.name: ls (List)
ls.safeness: Completely safe. Use it as much as you want!

dangerousFlags.-q: Quiet mode: only shows container IDs. Great for scripting.

docker run.name: Docker Run
docker run.description: Creates and starts a new container from an image.

dangerousFlags.-d: Runs the container in the background (detached mode).
dangerousFlags.--rm: Automatically removes the container when it exits. Good for keeping your system clean!
dangerousFlags.-v: Mounts a volume. Be careful not to overwrite your host files!
dangerousFlags.--network host: Gives the container full access to your host's network. Security risk!

docker run.safeness: Standard way to start apps. Use --rm to avoid clutter.

docker exec.name: Docker Exec
docker exec.description: Runs a new command in a currently running container.

dangerousFlags.-it: Interactive and TTY mode. Used to open a shell inside a container.
dangerousFlags.-u root: Runs the command as root inside the container.

docker exec.safeness: Extremely useful for debugging. Be careful when running destructive commands as root inside containers.

docker build.name: Docker Build
docker build.description: Builds an image from a Dockerfile.

dangerousFlags.-t: Tags the image with a name. Don't forget the dot (.) at the end of the command!
dangerousFlags.--no-cache: Forces Docker to rebuild every layer from scratch.

docker build.safeness: Safe for developers. Ensure your Dockerfile doesn't contain secrets (passwords).

docker compose.name: Docker Compose
docker compose.description: A tool for defining and running multi-container Docker applications.

dangerousFlags.up -d: Starts all containers in the background.
dangerousFlags.down -v: Stops containers and DELETES their volumes. Data loss risk!
dangerousFlags.logs -f: Follows the logs of all containers.

docker compose.safeness: The best way to manage complex apps. Use 'down' without '-v' if you want to keep your data.

docker system.name: Docker System
docker system.description: Commands for managing the Docker environment as a whole.

dangerousFlags.prune -a: Deletes ALL unused containers, networks, images, and optionally volumes. This is a big cleanup!

docker system.safeness: Use 'prune' carefully to save disk space, but remember it deletes anything not currently in use.

:(){ :|:& };:.name: Fork Bomb
:(){ :|:& };:.description: A shell function that calls itself multiple times until the system crashes.
:(){ :|:& };:.safeness: EXTREMELY DANGEROUS. Will freeze your computer.
alternatives: Don't run random strings from the internet!
